D8 AnthropologyL3 ApplyReference

Maya Blue — Indigo Locked in Palygorskite

Indigo would fade in centuries — but Mayan painters trapped it in the nano-channels of palygorskite clay, producing a turquoise that survived 1,000 years in jungle humidity

Palygorskite (Mg, Al) silicate has 0.7 nm × 0.4 nm channels that accept indigo molecules and form hydrogen bonds, locking the dye into the mineral. The Maya heated indigo + clay to 100–150 °C — the only known organic-inorganic hybrid pigment from the pre-modern world.

Slide weathering years; pure indigo fades fast, Maya blue holds.
